data.title: SoFi Technologies: Diversification And Strength Make It A Buy
data.description:
SoFi Technologies (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:SOFI) gave investors a volatile ride after releasing its first quarter 2025 earnings report on April 29, 2025. On the surface, the company produced an excellent quarter. Initially, the stock jumped 10% to reach a high of $14.51 before dropping to a price lower than before earnings the next day.

The decline is likely due to investors not liking SoFi's Technology division losing a significant customer. In a previous article on this company, I discussed the risk of the market valuing it more like an online bank rather than giving it a fintech premium. The loss of a Technology customer exacerbated some fears that SoFi's technology investment may fail to pay off.

Investors already have concerns about how its banking operations will perform in 2025. My last article on SoFi Technologies expressed optimism for its lending business in 2025. My exact quote:

A recession is probably the most significant risk to SoFi's lending business due to decreasing loan demand and increased loan defaults. With the probability of a downturn abating, optimism for SoFi's lending business is rising.

Unfortunately, that assessment failed to consider the current administration's plan to institute tariffs, which could cause inflation and a recession. In early April, JPMorgan Research raised the odds of a recession to 60%. The company's loan business could potentially turn sour if the economy deteriorates. Still, despite the recent stock decline, there are compelling reasons for long-term investors to purchase the stock.

Management has built the company to withstand difficult times by diversifying its business across lending, a robust financial services platform (SoFi Money, SoFi Invest, SoFi Credit Card, SoFi Protect), and a technology platform (Galileo). This diversification can help protect it in an economic downturn that precipitates declining loan originations, as the company can rely on fees from increased demand for budgeting tools or lower-cost investment options on the financial services platform, which could partially offset a declining loan business. Management has also strategically shifted to more fee-based revenue over the last several years, which is less economically sensitive than receiving net interest income (NII) from loans. So, while SoFi will likely suffer like all companies in an economic downturn, its fee-based businesses are built to survive a recession and thrive from the eventual recovery.

This article will discuss one of SoFi's fee-based revenue services, the Loan Platform Business (LPB). It will also review the first quarter 2025 results, examine the Technologies division's customer loss, valuation, and other risks, and explain why SoFi Technologies remains a buy.

SoFi's LPB as a growth driver

SoFi's LPB is a segment of its operations that facilitates loan originations for third-party investors. LPB generates fee-based revenue, which is less capital-intensive than holding loans on SoFi's balance sheet and doesn't present additional credit risk. It has become a significant revenue growth driver.

As its primary business, SoFi originates Prime loans to hold on its books or sell to third parties. The company has more demand for Prime loans than it can safely carry on its balance sheet. So, management created LPB as a side business, referring excess pre-qualified potential borrowers to loan origination partners. SoFi earns a referral fee for this service. The company also began underwriting and originating loans using funding from partner investors. The partner investors own the loans that SoFi originates on its platform. SoFi earns origination fees and may retain the servicing rights for these loans, generating ongoing servicing revenue. Originally, LPB was only for Prime loans. However, the company plans to extend LPB to non-prime loans eventually. SoFi Chief Executive Officer Anthony Noto said on the company's first quarter 2025 earnings call:

Another key contributor to growth in Financial Services is our loan platform business where we produce loans on behalf of third parties. In less than a year, we've grown the business to an annualized run rate of over $6 billion of originations and more than $380 million of additional high margin, high return fee based revenue. And we continue to see strong demand for these loans. This year, we finalized a $5 billion deal with Blue Owl, a $2 billion extension with Fortress and a $1.2 billion joint venture between Fortress and Edge Focus. These additional deals will accelerate the quarterly originations well above the current level of $1.6 billion. Importantly, the LPB loans do not present any ongoing credit risk and contribute to member growth as we hold the relationship and can benefit from cross buy into other SoFi products. So far, the vast majority of our LPB loans have been within our prime credit box, but over and above the volume we want to put in our balance sheet. However, we have the ability to also meet demand from buyers who want near prime loans with higher WACC [weighted average cost of capital], we can even extend this program to other loan types in the future. We are incredibly optimistic about the depth and breadth of the growth opportunities in the loan platform business, and we see it becoming our third billion dollars revenue business.

Blue Owl is short for Blue Owl Capital (OWL), and Fortress is short for the private company Fortress Investment Group.

Higher WACC loans are higher-risk, higher-return loans. In other words, investors receive higher interest rates on near-prime loans because of the higher risk of default. Some investors may diversify their portfolios by including higher-yield, higher-risk assets like near-prime loans to enhance their portfolio's overall return. This potential expansion of LPB is good for SoFi investors and is a reason to invest in the stock despite the potential worsening economic environment.

LBP fee-based revenue provides higher margin revenue than that generated by holding loans on its balance sheet and bearing the associated funding costs and credit risk. The growth of the LPB's higher margin and capital-light fee income should help improve the company's long-term profitability, which is attractive to investors.

Although it seems logical that a declining economy would adversely impact LBP revenue, the effect on the LPB isn't entirely straightforward and could be mitigated by several factors. Some institutional Investors may consider SoFi's prime and super-prime borrowers more desirable to invest in during a downturn due to their lower default rates in comparison to sub-prime borrowers. Additionally, the Federal Reserve is likely to lower interest rates during a recession, increasing demand for refinancing existing loans to reduce monthly payments. SoFi's LPB could facilitate these refinancings for its partners, like Blue Owl. Some institutional investors might see a counter-cyclical opportunity to invest in Prime consumer credit assets at potentially more attractive yields during a recession, viewing it as a long-term investment. SoFi's established platform and advanced origination capabilities could make it a partner of choice for these investors. Depending on the severity and nature of a recession, institutional investors may shift their investment priorities from deteriorating asset classes to more stable prime or super-prime consumer credit investments, potentially benefiting platforms like SoFi's with a solid track record for predicting defaults and an established lending infrastructure.

Company fundamentals

SoFi's consolidated non-GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) adjusted net revenue increased 33% to $770.72 million, beating analysts' estimates of $31.81 million. This revenue growth was its fastest in five quarters. Adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) increased 46% to a record $210 million at a 27% adjusted EBITDA Margin. Its adjusted earnings-per-share ("EPS") was $0.06, beating analysts' estimates by $0.03.

SoFi's Financial Services segment, which encompasses a range of products and services to manage members' money beyond just lending, remained the fastest-growing portion of the company's business. The Financial Services segment revenue increased by 101% year-over-year to $303.12 million. Management forecasts similar revenue growth and potential acceleration in the second quarter. Financial Service NII, which primarily generates revenue through deposits and credit card loans, increased 44.2% to $173 million. Financial Services segment noninterest income, which includes LPB, interchange, and brokerage fees, increased 319% to $130 million.

The Financial Service Segment comprises LPB, SoFi Money, and SoFi Invest. This article discussed LPB in the previous section. SoFi Money comprises high annual percentage yield (APY) savings and checking accounts, debit, and credit cards. It also offers various other services, including P2P payments, bill pay, vaults, automating recurring money moves through autopilot, money transfer through Zelle, a two-day early paycheck, $3 million of FDIC insurance, and more features. Management expects SoFi Money to become its second $1 billion revenue business. It built this business on its strong deposit base of $27.3 billion. After SoFi officially became a bank in February 2022, it used the deposits to lower its funding expenses for its loans and credit business by an estimated $515 million per year.

SoFi Invest is the company's platform for investing in various investment products, including individual stocks, options, and Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs). It also offers access to cryptocurrency and investment opportunities typically less accessible to retail investors, such as private equity, private credit, private real estate, venture capital, long-short hedge funds, and investments in private individual companies (like SpaceX and Anthropic).

The first quarter of 2025 saw the Financial Service segment's contribution margin expand by 23 points to 49%. This rapid expansion of profitability is likely due to the rapid growth of the high-margin LPB revenue. The contribution margin is a profitability metric that tells investors how much revenue from each segment sale contributes to covering a business's fixed costs and ultimately generating profit.

SoFi's Technology Platform segment is a business segment formed from its acquisition of Galileo and Technisys. The segment provides the underlying infrastructure and software solutions that power digital financial services for SoFi's products and third-party clients. It separates SoFi from being only an online bank to being a fintech.

SoFi's Technology segment only increased 9.6% year over year to $103.43 million. It produced a contribution profit of $31 million at a contribution margin of 30%. These numbers disappoint some investors, who no longer view the segment as a growth engine. It has the lowest revenue growth and the lowest profitability.

The Lending segment's adjusted net revenue increased by 26.7% year over year to $413 million, and the contribution margin declined six points to 58%. CFO Chris LaPointe said on the earnings call, "These strong results were primarily driven by growth in net interest income, which increased 35% year-over-year to $361 million."

The company's total loan originations increased by 66% year over year to $7.2 billion in the first quarter of 2025, a record.

Its first-quarter 2025 personal loan originations increased 69% year over year to $5.5 billion, with $1.6 billion originating through LPB on third parties' behalf. SoFi's first-quarter 2025 student loan originations increased by 59% over the previous year's comparable period to $1.2 billion. The company's first-quarter 2025 home loan originations increased 54% year over year to $518 million.

SoFi's capital markets activity refers to the company's buying, selling, and securitization of loans. If capital markets activity dramatically declines, the economy will likely be in terrible shape. However, capital market activity was robust in the first quarter. SoFI sold and transferred $3.1 billion of personal and home loans that it originated, including LPB loans. Out of the $3.1 billion total loans, $1.1 billion comprised personal loans, in which it sold the whole loan to an investor, not just a part of it or a security backed by it. The company sold these loans for an average price of 6.2% more than their face value. When SoFi sold these personal loans, it received cash from the buyers close to the loan's original amount (the "par" value). A significant part of the extra 6.2% SoFi got above the par value comes from agreements where SoFi would handle the loans' servicing (collecting payments and managing customer service) for the loans' new owners. SoFi immediately accounts for the expected income from these servicing fees by treating it as an asset ("capitalized"). These sales included a "small loss share provision," which incentivizes buyers to purchase the loan by agreeing that if a small percentage of these sold loans default, SoFi would have to absorb a portion of those losses. Still, this potential loss is immaterial compared to the much larger risk they would have had if it had kept all those loans on its books and those borrowers had defaulted. Selling these loans reduces SoFi's overall risk significantly.

In the first quarter, the company sold $322 million in home loans on its capital markets for 2.1% more than their original value. SoFi's ability to sell its personal and home loans at a premium to their original value indicates solid demand and good pricing power. Despite the worries over a worsening economy, it hasn't yet shown up in SoFi's capital market results.

SoFi also sold $90 million of late-stage delinquent personal loans, with borrowers over 90 days behind on their payments. The company made more money by selling these bad loans now, even at a discounted price, than it would have if management had waited until it officially declared the loans a loss and written them off. At that point, the chance of recovering any money is negligible.

The company also executed a $698 million loan securitization through its LPB. CFO Chris LaPointe said on the earnings call:

This was the first securitization of new collateral in our SoFi consumer loan program or SCLP since 2021 and the first using collateral originated in the loan platform business. Importantly, this channel provides our partners with meaningful liquidity to support their ongoing investment in the loan platform business. The transaction priced at an industry leading cost of funds levels with a weighted average spread of 87 basis points and an all in yield of 5.1%.

This commentary means that SoFi's LPB investors, like Blue Owl Capital and Fortress Investor Group, benefit when SoFi bundles and sells these loans as securities; it allows those partners to get their initial investment back more quickly. The securities are priced attractively for investors, with a 0.87% extra return above a benchmark interest rate to compensate them for the risk of investing. An "all in yield of 5.1%" is the total return investors can expect to earn by investing in these securities, including the benchmark rate and the spread. This yield was attractive enough to attract investors at a reasonable cost for SoFi.

CFO Chris LaPointe said the following about the consumers' credit health on the first quarter earnings call:

The health of our consumer remains strong. Despite the market volatility, we are not seeing signs of weakness. Our personal loan borrowers have a weighted average income of $158,000 and a weighted average FICO score of 743, while our student loan borrowers have a weighted average income of $134,000 with a weighted average FICO score of 769. Our credit trends continue to be strong after seeing delinquencies peak one year ago in the first quarter of 2024. For personal loans, the on balance sheet 90 day delinquency rate was 46 basis points in the quarter, a decrease of 9 basis points sequentially. The annualized charge off rate also declined to 3.31% versus 3.37% in the prior quarter. Had we not sold any late stage delinquencies, we estimate that including recoveries between 90 and a 120 day delinquent, we would have had an all in annualized net charge off rate for personal loans of approximately 4.8% versus 4.9% last quarter.

SoFi's first-quarter on-balance sheet student loans 90-day delinquency rate remained flat at 13 basis points ("bps") compared to the fourth quarter, while the annualized charge-off rate decreased 15 bps to 47 bps. Management is still confident in its initial estimate that the maximum the company will lose is 7% to 8% of all the money the company has lent out in personal loans during the entire time those loans are outstanding—a safety margin or the highest level of losses the company has prepared to lose. The company's recent loan vintages, originating from Q4 2022 to Q2 2024, have net cumulative losses of 4.09%, with 43% of the unpaid principal balance remaining. The current performance suggests that SoFi's lending practices are working well.

In the first quarter, total assets grew by $1.5 billion sequentially, driven by $1.6 billion of loan growth and approximately $265 million of growth in cash, cash equivalents, and investment securities, partially offset by a reduction in other assets. Total companywide cash and equivalents at the end of the first quarter was $2.7 billion. Regarding liability, in the first quarter of 2025, total deposits grew by $1.27 billion sequentially to $27.25 billion. CFO LaPointe said on the earnings call:

Net interest margin was 6.01% for the quarter, up 10 basis points sequentially reflecting our ability to sustain healthy deposit versus lending betas. This included a 27 basis point decrease in cost of funds, which was partially offset by a 13 basis point decrease in average yields. We continue to expect a healthy net interest margin above 5% for the foreseeable future.

The net interest margin ("NIM") is the difference between the interest income a bank earns on loans and other interest-generating assets and the interest expense a bank pays on deposits and other borrowings, expressed as a percentage of interest-earning assets. Think of the NIM as the loan's gross margin. The higher the NIM, the more profitable the lending activities. SoFi's NIM increase of 0.10% (10 basis points) sequentially is a positive trend. A "healthy deposit versus lending beta" for a bank generally means that when market interest rates changed, SoFi could keep the rates it paid on deposits relatively low compared to the rates it charged on loans. A NIM above 5% is a healthy profitability.

The company's end-of-quarter total capital ratio of 15.5% remains well above the regulatory minimum of 10.5%. Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) Capital is the highest quality capital a bank has, and it's the measure that regulators look at the hardest. It represents the bank's most reliable and readily available resources to absorb losses without ceasing operations. It's the core of a bank's financial strength, including common stock and retained earnings. CET1 risk-based capital is 15.3%, well over the required minimum of 7.0%.

Tier 1 capital is high quality but is considered slightly less robust than CET1's ability to absorb losses. It includes instruments like Perpetual Preferred Stock and certain forms of subordinated debt that can convert to equity. SoFi's tier 1 capital ratio of 15.3% is well above the required minimum of 8.5%.

The Tier 1 Leverage Ratio compares a bank's Tier 1 capital to its total leverage exposure (Total assets, not weighted by risk). Its Tier 1 leverage ratio of 13% is well above the minimum of 4.0%.

On the back of the company's solid quarterly performance, management raised its full-year 2025 guidance. Management raised its adjusted net revenue guidance to between $3.235 billion and $3.310 billion from its prior range of $3.2 billion to $3.275 billion. The raised guidance beat analysts' full-year 2025 estimates before earnings of $3.19 billion. After earnings, analysts raised their 2025 estimates to $3.26 billion. Management also raised its EPS guidance to between $0.27 and $0.28, above prior guidance of $0.25 to $0.27.

Management forecasts that the second quarter's adjusted revenue will be between $785 million and $805 million. The midpoint of the adjusted revenue guidance of $795 million was below analysts' estimates before earnings of $797.39 million. Analysts raised adjusted revenue estimates after earnings to $800.07 million. Management also forecasts second-quarter EPS of between $0.05 and $0.06. Analysts estimated that EPS before earnings matched the upper end of management's guidance of $0.06.

The Technology platform loses a customer

SoFi's technology platform measures its growth using the total number of user accounts that rely on Galileo's technology for their digital money activities, measured in millions, rather than the total number of financial institutions using its Galileo product or the total number of members using its products (one member may have several accounts).

Technology segment accounts declined 6% year over year to 158 million. Chief Financial Officer Chris LaPointe said on the earnings call:

This [account decline] was driven by a large client's decision last year to diversify their processing providers, which was factored into our outlook for 2025. We continue to diversify our client base by winning new business, and we expect these wins to drive financial performance and an offset in 2026.

Chris LaPointe's commentary refers to the company's Galileo platform losing Varo Bank on August 6, 2024, to rival technology platform Marqeta (MQ). A press release announcing Varo Bank's switch of technology providers stated:

Marqeta (NASDAQ: MQ), the global modern card issuing platform that enables embedded finance solutions for the world's innovators, today announced it has signed a five-year deal with Varo Bank, N.A., the first nationally-chartered consumer techbank in the U.S., to become its issuer processor. Varo Bank offers services such as fee-free checking accounts, free ATM access at over 40,000 locations, cash advances and lines of credit, secured credit-building credit cards, instant payment solutions andhigh yield savings accounts to customers, with over five million cards in-market.Varo selected Marqeta for its ability to combine sophisticated virtual, tokenized and physical card-issuing technology with faster speed-to-market which will help its customers improve their daily relationship with money.

SoFi's technology platform lost an estimated five million accounts with Varo Bank's move, which unnerved some investors, who questioned whether the Galileo platform could distinguish its service from competitors in the marketplace. It also raised another worry among some investors that some fintechs or traditional banks may avoid SoFi's technology platform because SoFi is technically a competitor in financial products. It is similar to the dynamic of retailers like Walmart (WMT), who don't want to use AWS as a cloud provider because it aids a competitor, Amazon (AMZN). As stated earlier in this article, losing a significant technology customer raised fears that the market will begin valuing SoFi as an online bank rather than an innovative, rapidly growing fintech.

The good news is that despite losing accounts, the company expanded its revenue. CFO Chris LaPointe said on the earnings call, "We continue to diversify our client base by winning new business and we expect these wins to drive financial performance and an offset in 2026." So, investors shouldn't consider the Technology platform a bad investment yet.

Valuation

One popular way to determine a bank's risk and stability is its tangible book value ("TBV"), which is the company's book value, excluding the value of intangible assets like goodwill and patents. Based on the value of its tangible assets, which represents the theoretical amount a shareholder would receive if management liquidated the company, the higher a bank's TBV, the greater its ability to withstand financial shocks. Banking investors sometimes value a bank based on the price-to-TBV. SoFi's price-to-TBV is 3.06, well above its three-year median of 2.163, suggesting overvaluation.

The following comparison of SoFi to other banks shows that it has the lowest tangible book value but the highest price-to-TBV, suggesting the market may be valuing it as a growth stock by pricing in its revenue and EPS growth potential rather than its current financial strength, a possible excellent position to take if investors consider it a fintech business entering a bull market. However, investors should remember that in the event of a recession or disruption in the capital markets, investors may value safety more than growth and decide to value it by dropping its price-to-TBV valuation to its three-year median of 2.163. Based on its price-to-TBV ratio, a conservative segment of the investing world may consider SoFi grossly overvalued. Be aware that Ycharts has yet to update SoFi's TBV, which is $ 5.061 billion instead of $4.615 billion on the chart. All other numbers are up to date.

SoFi Technologies' trailing 12-month price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio is 29.86, well above the financials sector median of 12.83, suggesting overvaluation. However, investors tend to value stock more on its forward-looking metrics than on the past. SoFi's 2026 forward P/E to growth ("PEG") ratio is 0.29 (2026 forward P/E ratio of 24.78 divided by EPS growth rate of 82.66%). Generally, the market considers a PEG ratio below one as undervalued. Suppose SoFi sold at a 2026 PEG ratio of 1.0; its price would be $42.15, a 228% rise over its May 5 intraday stock price of $12.82. However, considering the company's risks and market sentiment, investors shouldn't expect the stock to rise to such levels soon.

The following table compares SoFi to several fintech companies based on their one-year forward P/S ratio to estimated revenue growth ratio. Generally, investors will value fintechs with large lending operations based on their profit margins and revenue growth rates.

Company Name One-year forward P/S ratio One-year forward estimated revenue growth One-year Forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth TTM Net Margin One-year forward EPS growth estimates
SoFi 4.30 24.99% 0.17 17.22% -28.06%
Upstart Holdings (UPST) 4.73 57.98% 0.08 -20.45% ---------
Adyen (OTCPK:ADYYF) 18.88 32.49% 0.58 41.56% ----------
Robinhood Markets (HOOD) 11.85 22.60% 0.52 48.77% -18.17%
Nu Holdings (NU) 4.18 24.55% 0.17 23.85% 29.15%
PayPal (PYPL) 2.00 2.80% 0.71 14.26% 8.84%

In this comparison, PayPal is an outlier because it's a large, stable, mature company, and investors value it more for its stability and competitive advantages than its revenue growth rates and profitability. Outside of PayPal, investors value Adyen and Robinhood as the second and third highest because of those companies' net margins in the 40s, and because both companies don't have credit risk. Upstart is valued the lowest because of its negative profitability margins. Investors value SoFI and Nu Holdings similarly, even though Nu Holdings has a higher net margin. However, since Nu Holdings has credit risk in South America as opposed to SoFi's credit risk in the U.S. market, investors likely discount Nu because of the higher risk. Considering various factors, the market appears to value the stock appropriately based on its one-year Forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth rate.

My valuations don't include free cash flow-based valuations since banks have large non-cash items like loan loss provisions and changes in the fair value of financial instruments, which significantly impact net income but don't represent actual cash available to investors, as it does for industrial companies.

Other Risks

SoFi has rapidly expanded its balance sheet over the last several years, increasing its exposure to consumer credit risk during a turbulent economy that looked like it was recovering. It looked like a great decision at first. However, with the U.S. government's implementation of tariffs, the economy is in danger of deteriorating into recession, which could increase charge-offs and delinquencies. If the market sees SoFi fundamentals worsening, investors will likely become more concerned with its balance sheet and TBV than its forward PEG ratio. SoFi's price-to-TBV valuation may drop toward its median in a declining economy.

SoFi's lending segment relies heavily on loan sales and securitizations to maintain future revenue growth. If the market enters a recession, the capital market could seize up, and SoFi may find it challenging to securitize and sell loans. The company improved its balance sheet this quarter by selling $90 million of late-stage delinquent personal loans. Management may find it nearly impossible to get rid of bad loans in a similar fashion during a recession.

SoFi remains a buy

This company's stock price likely won't do well if inflation or a recession becomes a problem. As long as financial experts fill the news with worrying information about the economy, SoFi's stock price may have difficulty gaining traction. As a result, investors concerned about short-term performance (a year or less) should avoid this stock for now. However, as the company's forward PEG ratio suggests, the stock has a high upside in a recovering economy. SoFi is a perfect fit for long-term risk-tolerant growth investors. If you have the ability to look past the near-term clouds on the horizon, consider investing in SoFi Technologies. I maintain my buy recommendation for the stock.

data.title: SoFi Technologies: Supercharging Growth Via Capital-Light Digital Platform
data.description:
I’ve always liked SoFi Technologies (NASDAQ:SOFI) but couldn’t find the opportunity to write on it. Its innovative online platform, in my opinion, makes it a winner long-term. Now that the company is beginning to leverage its 10.9 million-strong member base to push a capital-light model as well as expand offerings, I think investors should consider this stock for their portfolios.

SoFi as a Platform Remains Strong And Growing

SoFi Technologies is an online finance platform with a wide array of services geared toward the younger millennial /Gen-Z audience. Its bread and butter used to be its refinancing student loan business, but it has since expanded to offer a wide array of different financial services.

SoFi’s main competitive advantage is its digital platform, which is very user-friendly and attractive to the aforementioned younger millennial /Gen-Z audience. The strengths of this platform are on its superior U/X and its streamlined digital experience, which is preferred by its target market over “old-fashioned”, more traditional banking. SoFi's main strength is that it is a “platform” type business that has a lot of positive Network effects. Readers of my articles know how much I like digital platform business because of its flywheel effects and ease of being able to scale rapidly.

These platforms create a virtuous cycle where more users in the ecosystem generate more activity and data, which in turn allows the cross-selling of new products and services. This cross-selling to a large core of users will enhance profitability by lowering overall member acquisition costs and allow the rapid scaling of revenue. In the company’s 10-K, SoFi has referred to this cycle as its “Financial Services Productivity Loop”.

The value of this platform is in its large number of members. The company recently announced that it had hit the 10 million member milestone, having 9x its member growth in less than 5 years. In 2024 alone, the company’s total members increased by 2.5 million, and in Q1 2025, memberships increased by an additional 34% year-over-year to a record 10.9 million members. The most recent increase in memberships translated to a product increase of 1.2 million new products, up 35% from the prior year, to a total of 15.9 million products.

These factors indicate that the membership growth trend remains intact and could still be accelerating as the company pushes more marketing efforts. These members have made good use of SoFi’s platform, having paid down $33 billion in credit card debt, borrowed $44 billion in refinanced student loans and $9 billion in funded home loans, as well earn more than $1 billion in interest on savings.

SoFi continuously strengthens its technology platform by offering new services that would delight its core audience. For example, with student loan repayments once again being a focus of the Trump administration, SoFi is giving its members more flexible options with the launch of SmartStart refinancing option. According to the press release, 71% of student loan borrowers delayed at least one major life event. With SoFi’s SmartStart refinancing, members can ease financial stress by keeping minimum monthly payments low while they go through major life changes.

The other interesting development is that SoFi is expanding its offering to the investing realm as well. New interesting features that would help SoFi members invest their funds were launched last year, in particular the SoFi robo-advisor platform and expanded access to alternative assets. In fact, SoFi has one of the broadest offerings of “alternative assets” thanks to its partnership with BlackRock (BLK).

There is strong demand for being able to invest in private companies, and SoFi is able to use its platform in order to fulfill this market need. In a survey of 5000 investors conducted, more than 87% have expressed interest in investing in privately held companies such as SpaceX, OpenAI, and Epic Games. These results are unsurprising as Gen Z investors are more likely to take risks with alternative assets. SoFi’s relationship with investment firm Templum makes this service a reality for the former’s members. The company has continued to lean into this unique feature of the SoFi platform with its recently announced access to a new class of the Cosmos Fund, with sole exposure to Anthropic, and other private equity funds such as Pomona Investment Fund and StepStone Private Markets Fund.

More importantly, these new services demonstrate the value of SoFi’s 10.9 million strong member platform and “Financial Services Flywheel” where new service offerings can continuously be added with the company being very responsive to members' needs.

SoFi as a Platform Has Numerous Advantages

There are many advantages in SoFi’s business model of being more a tech platform than a traditional bank. One of which is being able to operate and scale in a capital-light manner i.e., not being forced to keep loans in its Balance Sheet. Let me explain, most banks make loans and collect interest on those loans as it stay in the bank's balance sheet. SoFi does this as well with the loans that fit its risk and return profile, as doing so, it gains the benefit of recurring interest income like a traditional bank. But it also has the option to securitize a portion of its loans and sell it to asset buyers as well as host 3rd party loan businesses on its platform.

This platform approach to lending has a few advantages. As mentioned, SoFi is able to operate using a capital-light model, meaning that it is able to issue more loans than what is warranted by its balance sheet, as it is able to pass these on to 3rd parties for a fee. Secondly, this model allows SoFi to issue loans to members who may not necessarily fit the company’s credit risk tolerance and parameters. By still giving access to loans to these members who are higher risk, SoFi retains these members, and they continue to stay in the company’s ecosystem and generate fee revenue in other ways.

The company is continuing to expand and grow this model via its recently announced partnerships. One of the larger ones is the $5 billion partnership with Blue Owl Capital, a leading asset management firm with over $250 billion in assets under management. This two-year deal, the largest of its kind for SoFi, will use the Loan Platform to originate loans for Blue Owl to invest in. According to the company’s CEO, “This represents the largest single commitment for SoFi’s Loan Platform Business and is 2x our first commitment, enabling us to help more members get their money right while also diversifying toward less capital-intensive and more fee-based sources of revenue”.

This type of partnership seems to be successful for SoFi as it has expanded its late 2024 Loan Platform agreement with Fortress Investment Group by an additional $3.2 billion. This puts the total commitment of Fortress to SoFi at $5 billion in loan capital. These deals show the scalability of a capital-light lending model using SoFi's technology as a platform. Note that the earlier discussed expansion into investments is done via a “capital-light” approach as well.

Financial Analysis and Valuation

Looking at SoFi’s Q1 2025 and 2024 full year results shows a company that continues to grow. Full-year, the company reported $2.67 billion worth of revenue, an increase of 26% compared to the $2.12 billion reported in 2023. There has been a bit of slowdown in the first quarter of 2025. In Q1 2025, the company reported net revenue of $771 million, which is a 20% increase from the $645 million reported at the same time last year. Despite the slower revenue growth, the number of members grew at a much faster rate from 10.1 million to 10.9 million, with 800,000 new members being added in Q1 2025 alone translating to a 34% growth rate year-over-year. This would imply that revenue possibly would continue or accelerate on its growth trajectory as new members adopt SoFi’s products and services.

Management expects this growth to continue, and I believe that, given the factors discussed above, there is a good chance that it will. Guidance for 2025 is for net revenue of $3.235 to $3.310 billion, or approximately 24 to 27% year-over-year growth. Note that management has increased guidance from 23% to 26% despite the economic uncertainty of Trump’s tariffs, indicating confidence in the underlying factors of SoFi’s business model. Management is expecting adjusted EBITDA and GAAP Net Income of $875 to $895 million and $320 to $330 million, respectively.

What is even more encouraging is that within these results, SoFi’s non-lending segments are growing at a faster rate and now constitute a significant portion of 2024 revenue. Financial Services revenue in 2024 was $821.5 million a growth rate of 88% from the same time last year. This growth continued on in Q1 2025 as Financial Services segment net revenue of $303.1 million which was more than twice the reported figure from the prior year period. The main driver being SoFi’s Loan Platform Business, the potential of which I’ve highlighted earlier.

In 2024, its Technology segment revenue was $395.2 million increased by 12% year-over-year. In Q1 2025, the result was a segment revenue of $103.4 million and a slightly lower growth rate at 10%.

In total, Financial Services and Technology make up about 47% of SoFi’s total revenue in 2024 as the company pushes for a more fee-based, capital-light business model and away from relying on traditional lending. This percentage is bigger than the 38% reported last year, demonstrating the potential scalability and fast growth of these segments.

In terms of valuation, typically financial institutions are valued on a relative basis using their price to book ratio. In the charts below, I’ve selected a sample size of the top financial institutions that can be used as comparables for SoFi. I believe that this is a suitable method for valuation, as at the end of the day, SoFi’s core business is Financial Services, the technology aspect of it is just a means to deliver this service more efficiently and in a way that delights customers. From the Financial Services companies, I calculated an average Book Value of 1.87x Price to Book value.

At the end of Q1 2025, SoFi has a non-GAAP Tangible book value of $5 billion. As of writing this article the current price of SoFi is $12.86 translating to a market cap of $14 billion.

Calculating SoFi’s Price to Book value gives us roughly 2.8x. However, SoFi is growing at a much faster rate compared to its industry peers at 23.62% and 23.28% for current and forward estimated revenue growth, respectively. This is more than 3x the sector growth rate of 6.3% and 6.25% current and forward. So while SoFi’s Price to book value is roughly 50% more than its peers, the company’s growth rates are much higher. Therefore, it is in my view that SoFi deserves this premium valuation and is a Buy in my opinion.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Market Review

The first quarter of 2025 was a difficult one for technology stocks. The broader U.S. stock market transitioned from a "risk-on" environment to "risk-off," with the S&P 500® index returning -4.27% for the three months. Within this broad index, the sectors that performed best in Q1 were defensive ones and those related to commodities, such as energy. Information technology, on the other hand, delivered the second-weakest result, with only consumer discretionary faring worse.

What caused the weakness in tech? In general terms, post-election optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new Trump administration, disruption to government programs and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs.

Within technology, semiconductors – which had led the stock market higher for much of the first half of 2024 – was the hardest-hit group in the MSCI sector index this quarter, returning -18%. Relatively high valuations to start with in AI-related stocks and the uncertainty triggered by DeepSeek's (DEEPSEEK) announcement near the end of January were headwinds for the group. This company, a Chinese generative artificial intelligence startup, released a free large language model that was cheaper to develop, raising questions about spending on AI infrastructure and the valuations of the biggest players, including chipmakers. Meanwhile, semiconductor companies without exposure to AI continued to wrestle with excess inventories, following the dramatic shortages caused by the pandemic. As of March 31, this inventory glut has lasted more than two years but appears to be turning the corner to normalization.

In addition to the weak showing of semiconductors, several other key groups within the MSCI index struggled, including systems software (-11%), technology hardware, storage & peripherals (-12%) and application software (-12%). Although no industry segments in the MSCI index produced a positive return, internet services & infrastructure (-2%) and IT consulting & other services (-3%) came closest.

Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund produced a return of -14.26%, trailing the -12.68% result of the MSCI U.S. IMI Information Technology 25/50 Index and considerably behind the S&P 500®.

Turning to the fund, security selection and an overweight in semiconductors detracted meaningfully from performance versus the MSCI index. Picks in application software and systems software also hurt. Topping the list of the fund's individual relative detractors was an overweight position in Marvell Technology (MRVL)(-44%). In early March, the provider of data infrastructure and semiconductor solutions reported slightly better-than-expected fiscal Q4 earnings. However, in an environment of high valuations and elevated expectations for AI-related solutions providers, analysts said that the company's quarterly data center business results – which were in line with consensus views – likely proved somewhat disappointing and contributed to the stock's decline. That said, much of the downward pressure on shares came earlier in the year, when DeepSeek released its latest large language model. A sizable overweight in ON Semiconductor (ON)(-35%) also detracted in Q1. In February, the maker of semiconductors for automotive, industrial, medical and other applications reported Q4 financial results that included narrow misses on revenue and earnings. In addition, management issued a disappointing forecast for Q1, which some analysts viewed as a sign that weak demand for the firm's chips for autos had not yet bottomed and could be further hurt by new tariffs and policies that end or restrict tax credits for electric vehicles.

Conversely, stock selection in internet services & infrastructure and communication services contributed to our relative result. In stock-specific terms, Okta (OKTA)(+33%) was the fund's top relative contributor. Shares of the identity management company spiked higher in early March, following the release of its results for the fiscal fourth quarter ending January 31, which surpassed analysts' expectation. We reduced the fund's stake in Okta this quarter to lock in some profit, and it was the fund's No. 9 position as of March 31. Underweighting index component Broadcom (AVGO)(-28%) also contributed on a relative basis. Shares of the chip firm fell sharply after DeepSeek's announcement. The stock slipped further in advance of Broadcom's early-March announcement of financial results for the quarter ending February 2, which surprisingly topped analysts' consensus estimates for both revenue and earnings, driven by the company's AI semiconductor solutions and infrastructure software.

Outlook and Positioning

We begin the second quarter with an unusual degree of uncertainty about trade and tariff policy, as well as wide-ranging efforts to reduce the federal workforce. The stock market does not like uncertainty. What's more, the longer we remain in this uncertain state, the greater the potential for further damage to the stock market and the U.S. economy, in our view.

We believe that corporate earnings will likely be decent in the first quarter or two of this year, with the possibility that earnings will come in weak later in the year. The reason is that many purchases are being fast-tracked to get them in ahead of the tariffs, resulting in pulling forward demand for cars, smartphones and other items. One positive in all this is that we believe the administration is aware of the dislocations their policies are causing, and we think there's a good chance of a course correction before things get too bad. Then, too, much bad news has already been priced into stocks.

We continue to like the longer-term prospects for the companies in the portfolio, driven by powerful themes that remain in play, especially the investment in infrastructure and software needed to support the growth of generative AI and the evolving data center architecture. Other key trends include the growing importance of assuring adequate semiconductor supply, digital transformation, and the increasing chip content in electric vehicles, driven by further adoption of these vehicles and autonomous driving capabilities.

As a result of our bottom-up approach, the portfolio's largest industry overweight at quarter end – by a considerable margin – was semiconductors. In fact, five of the fund's six largest individual overweights as of March 31 were semiconductor stocks: Nvidia (NVDA), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), Marvell Technology, GlobalFoundries (GFS) and ON Semiconductor. Cisco Systems (CSCO), a provider of Internet Protocol-based networking and related communications equipment, accounted for the other overweight in this group of six. Because of its considerable head start over competitors, Nvidia remains our top choice among stocks that stand to benefit from generative AI as of quarter end.

On the other hand, application software and systems software ended the quarter roughly tied as the portfolio's largest industry underweights. We believe many software names have been bought based on overly optimistic assumptions about how quickly generative AI can be implemented, and we are waiting for better opportunities to buy.

The five largest underweights versus the sector index at the end of March were Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), Apple (AAPL), IBM (IBM) and Oracle (ORCL). As always, we thank you for your confidence in our stewardship of the fund, and in Fidelity's investment-management capabilities.

CHARACTERISTICS

data.title: Kroger Looks Expensive, But It's Still A Bargain: Here's Why We're Buying
data.description:
Kroger Co. (NYSE:KR) has been on a tear and is trading near an all-time high. Its share price has climbed nearly 36% over the past year and is up almost 20% in 2025 alone. At first glance, this might make it easy to dismiss the stock as expensive. But we believe that would be a mistake.

Our thesis is simple: Kroger is a buy because its earnings growth is underappreciated, its valuation remains compelling despite recent price strength, and its strategic execution across digital, private label, and alternative profits is driving durable upside. With a PEG ratio below 1.0, robust cash flow generation, and operational momentum, we believe there’s still value hiding in plain sight.

PEG ratio tells the real story

Investors often rely on the P/E ratio to judge valuation, and on that front, Kroger's trailing P/E of 19.9x might admittedly seem a touch on the lofty side—especially in comparison to its 10-year median P/E ratio of 15.1x.

But context matters. When we shift the focus to forward earnings and growth-adjusted multiples, the picture brightens considerably, as does our enthusiasm for the stock. Kroger's forward P/E is just 15.2x based on our estimates, and looking one year further out, we estimate that it drops below 14x. Even more compelling is its PEG ratio of just 0.83x.

As you can see above, that makes Kroger significantly cheaper than peers like Sainsbury (OTCQX:JSNSF) (1.31x) or Costco (COST) (4.92x), and largely in line with Sprouts Farmers Market (SFM).

A PEG ratio of this level suggests to us that the market isn’t fully pricing in Kroger's earnings power. In our opinion, it's a clear indicator that there’s value here, even at what appears to be a high share price.

Strong execution and operational momentum

Its share price may be high, but Kroger's fundamentals are anything but frothy. Its return on equity is an impressive 23.1%, and free cash flow clocked in at $2.8 billion for 2024. Management expects adjusted earnings per share in the range of $4.60 to $4.80 for FY 2025. We believe the high end of this guidance range is achievable and base our valuation on this.

That's because the company continues to scale high-margin revenue streams. In Q4, identical sales (ex-fuel) grew 2.4%, but digital sales surged 11% to reach over $13 billion for the year. Alternative profit businesses—including Kroger Precision Marketing and Health & Wellness—delivered $1.35 billion in operating profit, with 17% growth in retail media alone.

Kroger also introduced over 900 new "Our Brands" private label products last year. These proprietary offerings are priced below national brands but deliver higher margins and stronger brand loyalty. Over 90% of Kroger households now purchase Our Brands products, reinforcing the stickiness of its customer base.

Operationally, Kroger continues to improve supply chain efficiency. Dwell time in distribution centers is down, reducing spoilage and improving freshness. AI-driven inventory tools and automation in fulfillment centers (via Ocado partnerships) are helping boost margins in digital channels that were once margin dilutive. All this bodes well for the future and will be supportive of bottom line, in our opinion.

Share buyback and robust balance sheet

Another reason to be positive is that Kroger is putting its robust free cash flow to good use. In Q4 on 2024, it launched a $7.5 billion share repurchase program, including a $5 billion accelerated buyback expected to be completed by Q3 2025. That reduces the share count and directly boosts EPS.

And while some investors may prefer the company to pay down debt, we are comfortable with its current balance sheet. Kroger maintains a healthy Altman Z-score of 4.4 and a times-interest-earned ratio above 8.4x. While the debt-to-equity ratio of 2.16x is high, it is manageable given Kroger’s predictable cash flows.

It is also worth noting that management expects FY 2025 interest expense of $650 million to $675 million based on current interest rates. Should the Federal Reserve take rates lower, this could offer an additional tailwind to earnings which the market may be overlooking.

Why isn’t it fully rerated yet?

So why hasn’t the market fully rerated Kroger yet? Likely because headline metrics mask the underlying transition. Kroger is no longer just a traditional grocer—it is evolving into a digitally enabled, omnichannel platform.

While core grocery margins remain thin, Kroger is leveraging scale and technology to unlock value in alternative profit centers. These margin-rich businesses (retail media, pharmacy, health) are underappreciated in the current valuation, in our view.

Meanwhile, Kroger's ability to manage shrink, optimize sourcing, and improve store productivity means underlying profitability is stronger than it appears.

Valuation and peer comparison

Kroger currently trades at:

  • Forward P/E: 15.2x
  • EV/EBITDA (forward): 7.5x
  • PEG ratio: 0.83x

Peers such as Costco trade at 38x forward earnings and EV/EBITDA above 22x. Even Sprouts, which shares Kroger’s emphasis on private label, trades closer to 10x EBITDA.

Applying a 17x forward P/E to our EPS estimate of $4.80 implies a fair value of $81.60, ~11.8% above current levels. And including the impact of share repurchases, better than expected margin improvement, and possible interest cost tailwinds, we see potential for a share price in the mid-$80s over the next 12 months.

Risks

There are risks for investors to consider. These include margin pressure from pharmacy operations, inflationary volatility in fresh produce, and potential setbacks in digital margin expansion. The company is also facing legal and regulatory fallout from its now-terminated merger with Albertsons, which could result in further legal expenses or strategic distractions.

Additionally, elevated leverage from the recent debt raise for the buyback may limit financial flexibility if market conditions worsen. Consumer spending shifts in a slower economy could also impact higher-margin segments like health and wellness or media.

That said, we believe these risks are manageable and well-flagged, and Kroger’s conservative financial management history adds confidence.

Final Thoughts: A Defensive Stock with Upside Potential

Kroger is proving that stability doesn’t have to mean stagnation. Even with shares near a record high, we think the underlying valuation is still attractive when judged through the right lens.

Between its strong cash generation, margin-enhancing initiatives, capital returns, and a PEG ratio well below 1.0, Kroger looks well-positioned to deliver 8% to 11% annual total returns—just as management aims to.

For long-term investors, this grocery chain remains a staple worth holding. We are bullish on Kroger and believe this won’t be the last record high we see.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: WW International Declares Chapter 11
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

WW International (WW) sinks on Chapter 11 bankruptcy declaration. (00:21) Stock futures rise on news of U.S.-China trade talks. (01:15) Rivian (RIVN) unlocks an $1B investment from Volkswagen by recording another gross profit quarter. (01:49)

This is an abridged transcript.

WW International (NASDAQ:WW), formerly known as WeightWatchers declared Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection along with its first quarter results.

The company, which has recently been in financial trouble given the rising popularity of weight loss drugs, said that it is implementing a reorganization deal with certain holders to eliminate $1.15B in debt from its balance sheet.

This will not impact members.

The company also reported a 9.7% fall in first quarter revenue of $186.6M, while it recorded an adjusted loss of $0.47 per share.

"The decisive actions we’re taking today, with the overwhelming support of our lenders and noteholders, will give us the flexibility to accelerate innovation, reinvest in our members, and lead with authority in a rapidly evolving weight management landscape," said CEO Tara Comonte.

Stock futures are in the green early Wednesday after a government spokesperson said U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer would meet with their Chinese counterparts this week in Switzerland for preliminary trade negotiations.

Bessent and Greer will reportedly travel together to Geneva on May 8, also meeting with Swiss President Karin Ketter-Sutter to discuss negotiations over reciprocal trade.

Stocks have been volatile since President Trump announced his first round of tariffs on April 2.

Rivian Automotive (NASDAQ:RIVN) traded slightly lower on Tuesday after reporting Q1 results and updating on its 2025 outlook to adjust for the tariff backdrop.

Notably, the company said it achieved gross profit of $206 million during the quarter to mark its second consecutive quarter in the green. By hitting the milestone, Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN) unlocked an expected $1 billion investment from Volkswagen Group (OTCPK:VLKAF) (OTCPK:VWAGY) as part of their joint venture agreement. The investment is expected to be funded on June 30, 2025.

Rivian (NASDAQ:RIVN) also narrowed its quarterly adjusted EBITDA loss compared to a year ago and showed improvement with free cash flow.

Rivian Automotive (RIVN) is up 0.3% in premarket action.

The EV stock has outperformed Tesla (TSLA) and Lucid Group (LCID) in 2025 so far.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Trump teases 'very, very big' announcement, says 'not necessarily' on trade

Super Micro Computer projects $6B revenue for Q4 FY2025 with focus on AI and DLC solutions

Amazon's Zoox issues software recall, plans to scale up robotaxi production

Catalyst watch:

  • The NAMA Show in Las Vegas will be the center of attention of the convenience services industry. Attendees will see the latest in vending equipment, robotics, smart kiosks, holographic touchscreens, and AI-powered solutions. Companies showing off products include PepsiCo (PEP), Coca-Cola (KO), Hershey (HSY), and Nayax (NYAX). VenHub plans to show off its fully operational smart store.

  • At 4pm Costco (COST) will release its April sales report. Analysts will be looking to see if the retailer benefited from a stocking-up effect ahead of tariffs.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 0.8% at $59/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.1% at $97,000. Gold is down 0.6% at $3,377.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.3% and the DAX is up 0.1%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) -5% – Shares fell after the company reported mixed FQ3 results and lowered FY2025 revenue guidance.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 2:00 pm FOMC Announcement

  • 2:30 pm Fed Chair Press Conference

  • 3:00 pm Consumer Credit
